skill.name: google-maps-api-skill
skill.description: Collects structured public business information from Google Maps through the BrowserAct API, using keywords, language, and country inputs.
skill.summary: Collect Google Maps business listings with BrowserAct API automation.
skill.author: browser-act

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found network calls, environment API key access, command examples, and a critical combination heuristic. Review confirms the network and credential use are real but aligned with the BrowserAct Google Maps workflow; markdown command, reconnaissance, weak crypto, and obfuscation alerts are false positives. Publish with a warning about third-party data processing, secret handling, and compliance for automated business data collection.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party API Receives User Search Data
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE. The helper script sends keyword, language, and country inputs to https://api.browseract.com/v2/workflow and polls the same service for status and results. This is expected for this skill, but users should understand that their search terms and resulting workflow data are processed by BrowserAct.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct requests.post and requests.get calls target the BrowserAct workflow API. The behavior matches the skill purpose, so the risk is disclosure to a third-party service rather than confirmed malicious exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Environment API Key Used In Network Authorization
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE. The script reads BROWSERACT_API_KEY from the environment and places it in a bearer Authorization header for BrowserAct API calls. This is legitimate credential use for the advertised service, but it creates normal secret-handling risk if logs, prompts, or shell history expose the key.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable and Authorization header are explicit in the script. No evidence shows the key is sent anywhere other than the configured BrowserAct API endpoint.

medium_findings.title: Automated Google Maps Data Collection Requires Compliance Review
medium_findings.description: NEEDS_REVIEW. The skill is designed to collect business names, addresses, phone numbers, websites, ratings, and review counts from Google Maps. That can be legitimate research, but users must verify website terms, privacy obligations, and outreach rules before publishing or importing collected data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The collection purpose and output fields are stated in the documentation. The legal and policy impact depends on user jurisdiction and usage, so this is a compliance warning rather than proof of malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Are Expected Skill Usage
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The Ruby or shell backtick detections in SKILL.md are markdown inline code and a documented command example for running the local Python helper. I did not find evidence of hidden dynamic shell construction in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged tokens are documentation formatting and a plain Python invocation example. There is no subprocess, eval, or shell wrapper in scripts/google_maps_api.py.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Service Endpoints And Setup Links
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The hardcoded URLs point to the BrowserAct workflow API and BrowserAct console setup page. This is expected for a BrowserAct integration, although users should still review the vendor before sending data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The domains match the advertised service provider in the skill name and documentation. No unrelated webhook, paste site, or unknown collection endpoint was found.

low_findings.title: Static Reconnaissance And Weak Crypto Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The system reconnaissance, network reconnaissance, and weak cryptography alerts map to ordinary documentation text, status checks, and error handling. I did not find evidence of host probing, network scanning, cryptographic operations, or prompt injection in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line review shows descriptive text and simple response checks rather than reconnaissance or cryptographic code. The suspicious prompt-injection phrases listed in the evaluation instructions were not present.

low_findings.title: Critical Combination Heuristic Not Confirmed As Malicious
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The combination of network access, environment credential access, and script execution is real, but each part supports the declared BrowserAct API workflow. No evidence found of obfuscation, unrelated exfiltration endpoints, privilege escalation, or instructions to bypass security review.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The risky primitives are present together, so the heuristic is understandable. Semantic review ties them to a single advertised API integration rather than a concealed malicious workflow.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bearer Token Sent To External API
dangerous_patterns.description: The script builds an Authorization header from BROWSERACT_API_KEY and uses it for BrowserAct API requests. Treat the key as a secret and avoid pasting it into chat or logs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The Authorization header and request calls are explicit. The endpoint appears to be the intended vendor API, not an unrelated destination.

dangerous_patterns.title: Agent-Run Helper Script Performs Network Automation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill instructs the agent to run a Python helper that starts and monitors a remote browser workflow. This should be allowed only after the user understands the vendor dependency and data sharing.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The documentation and script both describe remote workflow execution. The pattern is inherent to the skill and should be disclosed rather than blocked.

content.value_statement: Finding local business data manually is slow and inconsistent. This skill runs a BrowserAct workflow to collect structured Google Maps listings from a keyword, language, and country.
actual_capabilities: Runs a Python helper that starts a BrowserAct Google Maps workflow.
Accepts keyword, language, and country inputs for local search targeting.
Uses BROWSERACT_API_KEY from the environment for API authorization.
Polls BrowserAct task status and reports running, failed, canceled, or timeout states.
Returns available business listing details such as names, categories, addresses, phones, websites, ratings, and review counts.
limitations: Requires a valid BrowserAct API key before any search can run.
Sends search inputs and workflow activity to the BrowserAct service.
Depends on BrowserAct availability, quotas, and Google Maps source behavior.
The reviewed script has unreachable result retrieval after polling, so output reliability should be tested before production use.

use_cases.title: Build Local Sales Lists
use_cases.description: Collect names, phone numbers, websites, and addresses for businesses that match a target category in a country.

use_cases.title: Compare Local Competitors
use_cases.description: Gather ratings, review counts, categories, and business status for nearby competitors in a selected market.

use_cases.title: Create Regional Directories
use_cases.description: Compile structured business listing data for directories, market maps, or internal research databases.

prompt_templates.title: Find Businesses By Category
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner search for one business type.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to find coffee shops in the United States. Use English results and summarize the available business names, addresses, websites, and ratings.

prompt_templates.title: Collect Contact Leads
prompt_templates.scenario: Basic lead generation with contact fields.
prompt_templates.prompt: Find dental clinics in Canada using English results. Return a concise lead list with business name, phone number, website, address, and rating when available.

prompt_templates.title: Research A Local Market
prompt_templates.prompt: Research Turkish-style restaurants in Germany. Compare categories, ratings, review counts, websites, and business status in a short market summary.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare CRM Import Notes
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow planning for sales operations.
prompt_templates.prompt: Collect real estate agencies in the United Kingdom. Group the results by completeness of contact data and flag records that need manual verification before CRM import.

output_examples.input: Find coffee shops in the United States with English results.
output_examples.output: A table-style summary of matching coffee shops, including business names, addresses, websites, phone numbers, ratings, review counts, and operating status when available.

output_examples.input: Research dental clinics in Canada for outreach planning.
output_examples.output: A prioritized lead list with available contact details, ratings, review counts, and notes about records that need manual verification.
A concise competitor overview showing listing names, categories, ratings, review volume, websites, and business status.

best_practices: Confirm that the user has a valid BrowserAct API key before running the helper script.
Use narrow keywords and country codes to reduce noisy or irrelevant results.
Verify collected contact data and compliance requirements before outreach or publication.
anti_patterns: Do not ask users to paste API keys into chat when an environment variable can be used.
Do not run broad repeated searches without checking service limits and data usage rules.
Do not treat scraped business data as fully verified without manual quality checks.

faq.question: Does this skill require an API key?
faq.answer: Yes. It requires BROWSERACT_API_KEY to be set before the BrowserAct workflow can run.

faq.question: What data can it collect?
faq.answer: It can return available business names, categories, addresses, phone numbers, websites, ratings, review counts, and status fields.

faq.question: Does it use Google Maps directly?
faq.answer: It uses the BrowserAct Google Maps API workflow rather than a local manual browser session.

faq.question: Is the data sent to a third party?
faq.answer: Yes. Search inputs and workflow requests are sent to BrowserAct, so users should review that service before use.

faq.question: Can it be used for sales outreach?
faq.answer: It can help prepare lead lists, but users must verify data quality and follow applicable outreach laws.

faq.question: Does it guarantee complete results?
faq.answer: No. Results depend on BrowserAct, Google Maps source availability, search terms, and the current script behavior.
